coredata.dc:title: Ultra small carbon dots from Crocus cancellatus as a highly fluorescence probe for synthetic Ponceau 4R dye sensing in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Esmail, Lawen Abdulsatar
Jabbar, Hijran Sanaan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, for the first time, natural Crocus cancellatus was adopted for the green, low-cost, and simple synthesis of highly fluorescent ultra-small carbon dots (CDs) with well-distributed size using a one-pot hydrothermal treatment. As-synthesized CDs are used as fluorescence sensing probe for quantitative analysis of Ponceau 4R (E124) synthetic food dye, which is widely used. However, excessive consumption of Ponceau 4R poses potential health risks. To obtain better-quality performance of as-synthesized CDs, the time and temperature of the hydrothermal carbonization reaction of crocus were optimized simply. The as-prepared carbon dots exhibit exceptional advantages including excellent fluorescence, satisfactory chemical stability, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. It produced a strong blue fluorescence when irradiated with UV light at 365 nm, which was subsequently effectively employed for Ponceau 4R sensing based on the quenching of fluorescence. The as-prepared CDs was characterized by EDX, XRD, FTIR, SEM, HRTEM, UV–Visible absorption spectrophotometry, and fluorescence. The effects of carbon dots fluorescence emission wavelength, CDs quantity, pH value, and reaction time were investigated to detect Ponceau 4R under optimum conditions. The synthesized carbon dot's fluorescence intensity allowed quantitative determination of Ponceau 4R in the linear range of 60–6000 nM with a detection limit of 40 nM that is lower than all previously published methods. Several soft drink samples were used to validate the potential use of this new fluorescent probe for P4R detection in actual samples with good precision and accuracy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Ultra-small
Carbon dots
Crocus cancellatus
Fluorescence
Ponceau 4R

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and structural characterization
Fluorescence properties of CDs and proposed sensing mechanism
Experimental conditions optimization for determination of P4R

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of (a) temperature and (b) time on the green-synthesis of CDs.
Synthesized CDs characterization: a) HR-TEM image, b) SEM image, c) XRD, d) Scale distribution histogram, e) EDX spectra, and f) FTIR spectrum.

FL spectrum of synthesized CDs (a) excitation from 300 to 360 nm, with 10 nm wavelength intervals; (b) quenching (turn-off) effects of Ponceau 4R solution; (c) illustration of FL quenching of CDs by P4R.
Optimization of FL quenching reaction conditions (a) CDs amount; (b, c) pH; and (d) incubation time.

(a) Fluorescence quenching of CDs with different Ponceau 4R dye concentrations; (b) respective calibration graph; (c) different components influence on the fluorescence intensity of newly synthesized CDs probe.
Intra-day and inter-day analysis for the evaluation of the accuracy and precision of the proposed FL method.
Comparison of the analytical performance of several quantum dots for determination of Ponceau 4R dye.
Determination of P4R dye in food samples by the standard addition method.

head.ce:title: Ultra small carbon dots from Crocus cancellatus as a highly fluorescence probe for synthetic Ponceau 4R dye sensing in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Salahaddin University-Erbil, Erbil, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Salahaddin University-Erbil, Erbil, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, for the first time, natural Crocus cancellatus was adopted for the green, low-cost, and simple synthesis of highly fluorescent ultra-small carbon dots (CDs) with well-distributed size using a one-pot hydrothermal treatment. As-synthesized CDs are used as fluorescence sensing probe for quantitative analysis of Ponceau 4R (E124) synthetic food dye, which is widely used. However, excessive consumption of Ponceau 4R poses potential health risks. To obtain better-quality performance of as-synthesized CDs, the time and temperature of the hydrothermal carbonization reaction of crocus were optimized simply. The as-prepared carbon dots exhibit exceptional advantages including excellent fluorescence, satisfactory chemical stability, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. It produced a strong blue fluorescence when irradiated with UV light at 365 nm, which was subsequently effectively employed for Ponceau 4R sensing based on the quenching of fluorescence. The as-prepared CDs was characterized by EDX, XRD, FTIR, SEM, HRTEM, UV–Visible absorption spectrophotometry, and fluorescence. The effects of carbon dots fluorescence emission wavelength, CDs quantity, pH value, and reaction time were investigated to detect Ponceau 4R under optimum conditions. The synthesized carbon dot's fluorescence intensity allowed quantitative determination of Ponceau 4R in the linear range of 60–6000 nM with a detection limit of 40 nM that is lower than all previously published methods. Several soft drink samples were used to validate the potential use of this new fluorescent probe for P4R detection in actual samples with good precision and accuracy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Green chemical synthesis is a process in which unsustainable chemicals and synthetic processes are replaced by sustainable, more environmentally friendly alternatives. Renewable energy, natural resources, and biocatalysts are frequently used in these systems to produce the required product. In many cases, green synthesis also produces more economical and sustainable results due to its use of natural resources and minimized waste [1]. Because natural food color can be lost during preparation, food colorants have been used to replace it. Food colorants are classified into two types: natural pigment and synthetic organic dye. Natural pigment may be taken from both plants and animals and is not hazardous to humans, although the majority of them are unstable and costly. A processed product has frequently been colored using synthetic organic dyes to restore or add color [2]. Synthetic organic colorants, on the other hand, can be detrimental to human health if taken in excess. Scientists have recently discovered that drinking soft drinks and eating sweets containing artificial ingredients might cause hyperactivity or harm intellectual development. The strawberry red azo dye Ponceau 4R (P4R) (trisodium salt of 1-(4-sulpho-1-napthyl-azo)-2-napthol-6,8-di-sulfonic acid) (E124) can be found in ice cream, jelly beans, sweets, sugar confectionery, beverages, wine, syrups, yogurt and other food products. About 4.0 mg/kg of body weight is the suggested upper limit of daily P4R consumption for humans [3,4]. P4R has the advantages of good stability, high solubility, low cost, and efficient coloring. Yet, studies have revealed that P4R can have harmful health effects, including cancer, neurobehavioral issues, and reproductive toxicity, when it is eaten in excess [5].

A CD, a carbon nanomaterial with a diameter less than 10 nm, consists of an oxidized carbon surface with a carboxyl group on top of an amorphous or crystalline core with a predominant sp2 carbon content [6]. Carbon dots are nanosized carbonaceous particles with attractive features such as tunable photoluminescence (PL), low toxicity, high chemical stability, simple preparation, and easy surface functionalization. These merits make CDs promising in the fields of sensing, bioimaging, catalysis, optoelectronics, and energy storage. Despite the great advances achieved in CD-based sensing of inorganic ions, organic explosive residues, and biomolecules [7,8]. CDs have a wide spectrum of absorption in visible light, as well as distinctive optical features and minimal toxicity [9]. CDs with particle sizes less than 3 nm were recently given a new term (ultra-small CDs) by researchers who achieved better control over particle size [10]. Due to their surface functional groups and unique optical properties, ultra-small CDs are often applied and achieve better results than CDs in imaging and sensing chemical species, such as ions [11], neutral or organic species [12], and bacteria assaying [13].

In the past several years, natural materials have been used in the synthesis of CDs from a range of raw materials. In contrast to general chemicals, natural materials are more readily accessible in nature, making CDs more suitable for industrial applications. In addition, as natural materials frequently include a variety of heteroatoms, CDs made from them are consistently packed with different surface groups and have distinctive features without any additional passivation or modification. Thus, different natural biomasses have been used to create CDs, such as tomato juice [14], cabbage [15], fish scales [16], rose-heart radish [17], solanum tuberosum [18], lotus root [19], etc.

Several reliable approaches for determining Ponceau 4R are used to verify food safety, including HPLC with various detectors [20,21], electrochemically methods using different modified electrodes [22], mass spectroscopy [23], and spectrophotometry [24]. These techniques are tried-and-true and frequently used, but they have several drawbacks, including time-consuming, specialized technical knowledge, relatively expensive equipment, and extensive sample preparation that prevent them from developing and expanding their application. Thus, it is necessary to develop a technique that is simple to use, fast, inexpensive, and highly sensitive for the quantitative determination of P4R dye. A good option for a highly sensitive probe would be fluorescence carbon dots. In recent years, quantum dots (QDs), a class of fluorescent nano-probes with the advantages of adjustable emission color and high quantum yield, have gained considerable attention for creating sensitive and selective fluorescence probes [5]. All of the investigations show that analyte and CDs surface interact with each other's, and this leads to changes in the physical properties of CDs surface [19].

To our knowledge, however, there have only been a few reports of Ponceau 4R dye detection using the fluorescence quenching of CDs. Thus, highly-fluorescent ultra-small carbon dots were synthesized by carbonizing Crocus cancellatus, which had been successfully used as a fluorescent sensing probe for the quantitative determination of P4R with high sensitivity and selectivity.

Citric acid, sodium citrate, glucose, sucrose, and ascorbic acid, Na2HPO4.2H2O, and NaH2PO4 (Sigma Aldrich). The Crocus cancellatus was obtained from Perbal Mountain, Kurdistan Region, Iraq. Ponceau 4R (E124) stock solution (1.0 mM) was prepared from standard powder obtained from AwaMedica Pharmaceutical Factory, Erbil, Iraq. While the stock solution was repeatedly diluted to obtain lower concentrations. All chemical compounds were of analytical grade and used without more purification. Throughout all studies, deionized water (DI) was used.

LKB-BIOCHROM 4050/4051 spectrophotometer were used for the UV–Visible absorbance spectra recording. A Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Kattan Co.) was used for fluorescent measurements. PHILIPS CM120, Netherland transmission electron microscope was used to take HR-TEM images. FTIR spectra were recorded using the IRAffinity-1 SHIMADZU spectrometer at 25 °C. The XRD pattern (X-Ray Diffraction) was done using a Philips instrument (PW, 1730) and an SEM images were obtained by TESCAN MIRA III (Czech).

Carbon dots were synthesized from Crocus Cancellatus as a carbon precursor using the hydrothermal technique, with certain modifications as reported in the literature [17]. One gram of fresh chopped crocus was added to 40.0 mL ultrapure water and stirred for 30 min in a conventional synthesis. After that, the mixture was transferred into a 50.0 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and then heated in an oven for 8 h at 180 °C. Finally, the resulting suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15.0 min before passing through a 0.20 μm micro-syringe filter. To obtain the optimal temperature for CDs synthesis, the same procedure was performed at 160, and 200 °C.

Samples of four different soft drinks were collected from local markets. They were heated for 5.0 min to eliminate any dissolved carbon dioxide (CO₂) and used further without any additional treatments [25]. Three different standard P4R dye solution concentrations were mixed with 1 mL of the sample solution that was diluted with phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7) and 0.5 mL of carbon dots solution for analysis using the suggested FL method.

The Ponceau 4R detection was carried out in a phosphate buffer solution with a pH of 7 at room temperature (25.0 °C). In a normal session, 4.0 mL of phosphate buffer was used to dilute 5 μL of purified carbon dots (1.0 mg/mL) solution and variable quantities of Ponceau 4R solutions with concentration range from 60 to 6000 nM. The fluorescence measurement was carried out after the usual incubation at 5 min intervals. To analyze fluorescence quenching for the interferences effect study, 5.0 μL of each interfering compound (sucrose, glucose, citric acid, sodium citrate, ascorbic acid, K+, Na+, Ca2+, NO₃−, and Cl−) with a concentration of 100 nM, while 50 nM of Cu2+ and Fe3+ was added to the carbon dots solution. A 10 nm slit width was used to record FL spectra at 330 nm and 415 nm of the excitation and emission wavelengths after 5.0 min of mixing [5,19].

Quinine sulfate was used as a reference standard to estimate the relative quantum yield of as-synthesized carbon dots and was taken into consideration by published methods. Quinine sulfate has a quantum yield of 0.540 at 360 nm in 0.1 mol/L sulfuric acid. The following equation was used to calculate the quantum yield's absolute value:
Qu . Yi =
Q R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n 2
n R 2
where (Qu. Yi) is the quantum yield, (I) is the integrated fluorescence emission intensity that has been measured, (A) is the absorbance, and (n) is the refractive index of the solvent. The recognized fluorescence standard's related parameter is indicated by the subscript R.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and structural characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Crocus cancellatus is the only precursor utilized in the synthesis of carbon dots. The synthesized CDs were well dispersed in water and formed a yellowish-brown color solution. The effect of temperature and time of Crocus cancellatus hydrothermal carbonization reaction were studied to obtain a better-quality performance for the prepared CDs.

The CDs were synthesized at different temperatures (160 °C, 180 °C, and 200 °C) to get the best synthesis conditions. UV–Visible spectra were used to characterize all of the synthesized CDs solutions. The ideal temperature for the synthesis of CDs was discovered to be 180 °C, as illustrated in Fig. 1a. When the temperature was raised from 160 to 180 °C, the spectra of carbon dots developed two different peaks at 224 nm and 280 nm, which is similar to the results obtained by Alarfaj and co-authors [26]. Extremely blue luminescence was visible under UV (365 nm) illumination, as seen in the inset of Fig. 1a.

It is essential to confirm that the conversion of Crocus cancellatus into CDs has occurred to completion under the experimental conditions before the separation of the final product during the synthesis of carbon dots. As a result, the conversion process at 180 °C was observed over time. The results at 5.0 h show that the conversion rate is low (Fig. 1b). As time increases, the conversion rate increases until it reaches its highest point at 8.0 h which indicates that all biomass-convertible has been transformed into carbon dots. When the reaction period is extended to 12 h, the rate of carbonization begins to somewhat decrease. This is because prolonged reaction periods cause the functional groups located on the surface of carbon dots to break down [27].

HR-TEM images were used to examine the particle sizes and morphologies of as-synthesized CDs, and the size distribution was estimated using Image J software. Typical HRTEM and SEM images of CDs are shown in Fig. 2a and b, respectively. Fig. 2a depicts a typical HRTEM image of as-synthesized C-dots, demonstrating their small size and tiny ball-shaped structure. The corresponding size histogram with CDs size distribution contained within 0.9–1.8 nm is shown in Fig. 2d. According to the HRTEM image, the average size of C-dots was 1.3 nm. [28–30]. The compositional characteristics and crystalline phases of CDs were further analyzed using XRD measurements. The C-dots, XRD patterns (Fig. 2c) show a broad diffraction peak at about 2θ = 23° (JCPDS cards No. 00–050-0926), indicating the amorphous nature of the green-synthesized carbon dots [31]. The interlayer spacing (d-spacing) of the CDs was calculated to be 0.38 nm. Moreover, there was good agreement between the distance between the two lattice planes as determined by HRTEM and the X-Ray Diffraction data [32].

EDX analysis was used to evaluate the elemental composition of CDs, and the results indicate presence of C (50.37 %), O (41.66 %), Na (0.47 %), Mg (0.51 %), Al (1.41 %), Si (0.42 %), P (1.67 %), S (0.63 %), Cl (0.54 %), and K (2.33 %) as shown in Fig. 2e. The as-synthesized CDs are mostly composed of carbon and oxygen atoms, which is indicated of CDs formation, according to the elemental composition. A minor amount of Mg, Na, Al, Si, Cl, S, P, and K was also found because of the use of Crocus cancellatus which may contain these elements [33].

The FTIR spectra of CDs (Fig. 2f) show that the broad peaks around 3437.15 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl group and absorption peaks at 1508.33 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of OH groups respectively [34,35]. The band at 1635.45 cm−1 indicated the existence of COO−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties of CDs and proposed sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized CDs exhibited typical excitation-dependent FL emission properties, which means wavelength-tunable emission, similar to several carbon dots previously characterized (Fig. 3a). The wavelength of the emission was changed slightly when the carbon dots dispersion was excited by the photo with the wavelength ranging from 300 to 360 nm. When the excitation wavelength increased from 330 nm to 360 nm, there was a noticeable redshift in emissions with decreased intensity, which confirmed the carbon dots' remarkable wavelength tunable ability. Excitation of carbon dots at 330 nm produced the maximum emission intensity [36,37].

It is clear from Fig. 3b that adding P4R standard dye caused the FL intensity to turn-off (quench) quickly. Carbon dots possess abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups, which interact with analytes and find numerous applications. The detection of different analytes was assessed by monitoring the initial FL intensity of carbon dots. It has been demonstrated that carbon dots' high selectivity for P4R is due to hydrogen bonding between them that forms complexes [5,19]. In the proposed system, Ponceau 4R that is a type of azo dye. It has three sulfonic groups (–SO₃−) in each molecule, and the carbon dots' surfaces have carboxyl groups (–COOH). As a result of the significant electronegativity of the oxygen atoms on the surfaces of the carboxyl and sulfonic groups, a complex of carbon dots with Ponceau 4R was formed through hydrogen bond. With the absorption of energy, the electron of this complex tunnels from its ground state to its excited state. The electrons of this complex then return to ground state as a result of the heat lost to the environment and the carbon dots quench (Fig. 3c) [38].

Fluorescence quenching usually originate from static or dynamic quenching in which the fluorescent molecule reacts with the quencher. Static quenching involves the formation of the complex by combining the ground state fluorescence molecule with the quencher while in dynamic quenching the collision of excited fluorescence molecule with the quencher. Furthermore, dynamic quenching and static quenching mechanism can be easily distinguished by careful examination of the absorption spectrum of fluorophore, since the absorption spectrum of fluorophore keeps constant during dynamic quenching process but changes during static quenching process. For the sake of precise revelation of quenching mechanism in this system, UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, P4R, and CDs-P4R systems were recorded. The absorbance peak of P4R was at 507 nm, The CDs-P4R system also showed the absorbance peak at 507 nm, it implied the formation of CDs-P4R complex it may be forms a complex between them through the hydrogen bonding, it met the quenching mechanism of CDs was static quenching. In addition, to prove the quenching mechanism of CDs was static quenching, the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs was measured to be 3.91 ns. When Ponceau 4R was added into the solution of CDs, the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs was measured to be 3.96 ns. The average fluorescence lifetime of CDs was almost unchanged in the absence or presence of P4R and indicate static quenching [39,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental conditions optimization for determination of P4R
ce:section.ce:para: First of all, amount of CDs optimized to give maxim fluorescence intensity from 5 to 200 μL as shown in Fig. 4a. Fluorescence intensity decrease from 100 to 200 μL due to self-quenched, while increase the CDs amount from 10 to 50 μL leads to increase the FL intensity to the out of scale. The intensity reach to the maximum value at 5 μL, therefore 5 μL chosen as optimum CDs amount.

The fluorescence quenching response between CDs and P4R were examined as a function of proposed sensor conditions such as incubation time and pH. The FL response and quenching degree were investigated at different pH values (4.0, 6.0, 7.0, 9.0, and 12.0) in the presence and absence of 300 nM P4R dye. Fig. 4b,c shows that at low pH values, the fluorescence quenching intensity is low, increases to its highest value at pH = 7, and then decreases with pH increasing. Thus pH 7 was selected for subsequent works.

Also, the incubation time (time required for the CDs and P4R to react completely) was examined at various time points (1 to 60 min). As shown in Fig. 4d, the CDs quenching response by 300 nM P4R began after 1 min, reached its peak after 5 min, and subsequently remained stable for about 60 min. The quenching reaction, which took just 5 min to complete, was determined to be quite swift and chosen as the optimum incubation time.

Fig. 5a shows the FL spectra of the green-synthesized carbon dots in the presence of different concentrations of P4R solution to evaluate the applicability and sensitivity of the new FL sensing probe toward P4R dye. It was evident that when the concentration of the dye increased, the fluorescence intensity at 415 nm steadily reduced. Fig. 5b shows a good linear relationship between the quenching efficiency (F°/F) and different P4R dye concentrations in the range between 60 and 6000 nM. The resulting equation for linear regression is F°/F = 0.0004C + 1.0259, where F° stand for the CDs FL intensity, F is CDs FL intensity in the presence of P4R dye, and C stands for the P4R dye concentration with the related determination coefficient of (R2 = 0.9989). Using the slope of the quenching efficiency graph (Ks) and the standard deviation of the blank solution (SDb) (n = 5), the limits of detection (LD = 3 × SDb / Ks) were calculated to be 40 nM.

To assess the competency of the chemosensor, statistical analysis of the method's accuracy and precision was performed. Using obtained optimum conditions, five repeat measurements of Ponceau 4R standard solutions (150, 1000, and 4000 nM) were carried out to evaluate the precision using relative standard deviation (RSD) values. While the recovery (R%) values were used to assess the method's accuracy as shown in.

Table 1 . The successful outcomes demonstrate the high accuracy and precision of the devised approach. As shown in Table 2 , this sensing technique performs better for P4R determination in terms of sensitivity, lower detection limits, and wide linear range, than other published fluorescent techniques.

One of the key elements influencing a method's effectiveness is selectivity, which is just as vital as sensitivity. The fluorescence change of the proposed probe with possibly interfering components such as ascorbic acid, sodium citrate, caffeine, citric acid, glucose, sucrose, and ions such as K+, Na+, Ca2+, NO₃−, Cu2+, Fe3+ and Cl− with relatively higher concentrations was studied to explore if this technique was specific for P4R dye detection in food samples. The impact of each component on the carbon dots fluorescence intensity was assessed separately. With the exception of P4R dye, the other components' impact may be disregarded. Fig. 5c depicts how just Ponceau 4R significantly reduced fluorescence (compared with the other). These results imply that the suggested approach has great P4R dye selectivity and may be used to measure P4R in real samples.

Any developed method's success depends on its applicability. As a result, using the standard addition method, the effectiveness of the new proposed FL probe was assessed in three separate soft drink samples and two commercial food colorant solutions that contains P4R synthetic dye.

Table 3 is a list of the analysis results. The P4R dye recoveries ranged from 98.20 to 100.187 %. Furthermore, the excellent reproducibility of this procedure was confirmed by the RSD value, which was determined to be less than 0.323 %. These results, which include excellent precision and accuracy, imply that the analytical performance for P4R dye quantification in soft drink samples is satisfactory. The approach ultimately proved to be extremely practical when P4R synthetic dyes were analyzed in real samples.

Ponceau 4R dye is one of the extensively used food colorings that may be harmful to human health, as was before mentioned. The development of an accurate, rapid, and sensitive P4R measurement technique is essential. Crocus Cancellatus was used as a carbon precursor for the synthesis of ultra-small CDs that was utilized as a FL quenching sensor for the quantitative study of Ponceau 4R, and its low limits of detection was as low as 40 nM. With acceptable results, the proposed FL probe was used to determine the P4R dye content in real samples. Above all, this fluorescence quenching sensor offers a technique that is reasonably practical and economical method, which can evaluate the exceeding P4R with a FL sensing. The calculated quantum yield was 26.4 %.
Hijran Sanaan Jabbar conceived and designed the experiments. Supervised all stages.
Lawen Abdulsatar Esmail performed the experiments, analyzed the data, and drafted the manuscript.
All authors read and approved the final manuscript.
This work wasn't funded in any way.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110334.
